---
title: "Child Sponsorship and Educational Aspirations"
subtitle: "Evidence from Rural Mexico — Summary"
author: "D. Prudencio"
institute: "M Csapek SA"
date: "Education Economics (2025)"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, emory-clean.scss]
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: fast
    incremental: false
    toc: false
    embed-resources: true
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    fig-align: center
bibliography: ../Bibliography_base.bib
---

## The Puzzle: Why Doesn't Education Pay Off Here?

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**The gap:** Returns to education are positive — yet [educational attainment stays very low]{.hi-gold}

- Adults in Oaxaca and Chiapas average only **7.5 and 7.2 years** of schooling — barely above primary
- Both regions among the poorest in Mexico

**Standard explanation:** [external constraints]{.negative} — poverty, distance to schools, poor health

**But also:** Growing evidence that [internal constraints]{.hi-gold} matter too

- Aspirations, self-efficacy, and self-esteem shape whether children *want* to pursue education [@Cunha2009; @Dalton2016; @Heckman2012]
:::

::: {.column width="37%"}
::: {.methodbox}
**Research question:**

Can an international child sponsorship program shift *the level of education* children aspire to reach?
:::

::: {.keybox}
**Compassion International (CI):** Third-largest child sponsorship program worldwide

≈**2.2 million** children across **29 countries**

In Mexico: **33,360** children in >185 centers
:::
:::
:::

## What Does CI Do? And How Do We Study It?

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Three program pillars:**

1. [Material support]{.hi-gold} — school supplies, uniforms, food, healthcare
2. [After-school program]{.hi-gold} — 5–6 hrs/week of academic tutoring and socio-emotional development (self-esteem, self-efficacy, life skills)
3. [Letter exchanges]{.hi-gold} with international sponsors — broadens career horizons and life possibilities

Average **9.3 years** of participation

*De facto*: school attendance required for continued sponsorship
:::

::: {.column width="47%"}
::: {.methodbox}
**The study:**

- 2017 survey, **Oaxaca and Chiapas**, Mexico
- **8 rural communities**: 4 with CI, 4 matched controls
- **403 children aged 12–15** (163 sponsored, 240 non-sponsored)
- Key outcome: Does the child *aspire* to a higher education degree?
:::
:::
:::

## How Do We Measure CI's Causal Impact?

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
**The challenge:** CI deliberately targets the [poorest]{.negative} children — sponsored and non-sponsored kids differ in ways we cannot fully measure

- Raw comparison of aspirations = [not causal]{.negative}

**The solution:** A [binary Roy model]{.hi} [@Aakvik2005]

- [Instrument ($Z$):]{.hi-gold} Age when CI arrived in the village — younger children more likely selected, but age-at-arrival should not affect current aspirations

Identifies [ATE]{.hi} (average child), [ATT]{.hi} (sponsored), [MTE]{.hi} (by disadvantage)
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
<svg viewBox="0 0 500 255" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:100%;max-height:205px;">
  <defs>
    <marker id="mn" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="9" refY="5" markerWidth="5" markerHeight="5" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 Z" fill="#555"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="mg" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="9" refY="5" markerWidth="5" markerHeight="5" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 Z" fill="#2E75B6"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="mr" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="9" refY="5" markerWidth="5" markerHeight="5" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 Z" fill="#b91c1c"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <!-- Node Z -->
  <rect x="22" y="59" width="116" height="52" rx="6" fill="#E8EDF5" stroke="#012169" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="80" y="79" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="#012169" font-family="sans-serif">Z</text>
  <text x="80" y="95" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#333" font-family="sans-serif">Age at</text>
  <text x="80" y="108" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#333" font-family="sans-serif">CI arrival</text>
  <!-- Node S -->
  <rect x="192" y="59" width="116" height="52" rx="6" fill="#E8EDF5" stroke="#012169" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="250" y="79" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="#012169" font-family="sans-serif">S</text>
  <text x="250" y="95" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#333" font-family="sans-serif">CI</text>
  <text x="250" y="108" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#333" font-family="sans-serif">Sponsorship</text>
  <!-- Node Y -->
  <rect x="362" y="59" width="116" height="52" rx="6" fill="#E8EDF5" stroke="#012169" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="420" y="79" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="#012169" font-family="sans-serif">Y</text>
  <text x="420" y="95" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#333" font-family="sans-serif">Higher-Ed</text>
  <text x="420" y="108" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#333" font-family="sans-serif">Aspiration</text>
  <!-- Node U (latent, dashed ellipse) -->
  <ellipse cx="250" cy="210" rx="90" ry="36" fill="#fff5f5" stroke="#888" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="6,3"/>
  <text x="250" y="207" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#555" font-family="sans-serif">U</text>
  <text x="250" y="222" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#666" font-family="sans-serif">Unobservables</text>
  <!-- Arrow Z→S (solid, navy) -->
  <line x1="138" y1="85" x2="186" y2="85" stroke="#555" stroke-width="1.6" marker-end="url(#mn)"/>
  <text x="162" y="78" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#555" font-family="sans-serif">↑ prob.</text>
  <!-- Arrow S→Y (solid, steel blue — effect of interest) -->
  <line x1="308" y1="85" x2="356" y2="85" stroke="#2E75B6" stroke-width="2.2" marker-end="url(#mg)"/>
  <text x="332" y="78" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#2E75B6" font-family="sans-serif">effect?</text>
  <!-- Arrow U→S (red dashed) -->
  <line x1="228" y1="176" x2="237" y2="115" stroke="#b91c1c" stroke-width="1.4" stroke-dasharray="5,3" marker-end="url(#mr)"/>
  <!-- Arrow U→Y (red dashed) -->
  <line x1="320" y1="185" x2="390" y2="115" stroke="#b91c1c" stroke-width="1.4" stroke-dasharray="5,3" marker-end="url(#mr)"/>
  <!-- Arc Z→Y excluded path (curved over top, red dashed) -->
  <path d="M 80 59 Q 250 5 420 59" fill="none" stroke="#b91c1c" stroke-width="1.4" stroke-dasharray="5,3" marker-end="url(#mr)"/>
  <text x="250" y="20" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="#b91c1c" font-family="sans-serif">✗</text>
</svg>

::: {.smallest}
The Roy model corrects for $U$ by exploiting variation in $Z$

[✗]{.negative} = exclusion restriction: $Z$ does not directly affect aspirations
:::
:::
:::

## Main Result: CI Raises Aspirations

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Effect on *sponsored* children (ATT):**

- Estimated [+17 to +20 percentage point]{.hi-gold} increase in the probability of aspiring to higher education
- Direction consistent with prior CI studies on adult outcomes [@Wydick2013]

**Statistical precision:**

- [Not statistically significant]{.negative} — small sample ($N=163$ sponsored) combined with a complex estimation model amplify uncertainty
- Results are *suggestive*, not definitive — but the direction is clear and meaningful
:::

::: {.column width="41%"}
::: {.resultbox}
**Back-of-envelope:**

If higher aspirations translate into behavior, a [20 pp increase]{.hi-gold} implies

≈ **8 more months of schooling**

Consistent with @Wydick2013: 1.0–1.5 additional years of education for adult CI participants
:::

::: {.keybox}
Even an imprecise result is informative: the program appears to be working in the right direction
:::
:::
:::

## Two Additional Insights

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
**[Insight 1:]{.hi} CI's targeting is efficient**

The Marginal Treatment Effect (MTE) shows that children [most likely to be selected]{.hi-gold} into CI are also those who [benefit the most]{.hi-gold} from it

::: {.resultbox}
**No efficiency-equity trade-off:** Aid programs often face a dilemma — targeting the most vulnerable may not reach those with the highest gains. [CI avoids this dilemma:]{.positive} the most disadvantaged children extract the greatest benefit
:::
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
**[Insight 2:]{.hi} A surprising gender gap**

- [Girls aspire more]{.hi-gold} to higher education than boys (boys are ≈12 pp less likely to aspire, among non-sponsored)
- Yet girls expect [≈50% lower income]{.negative} than boys at the same education level

::: {.keybox}
The gender gap in aspirations is **not** explained by income beliefs — structural barriers and identity formation dominate at ages 12–15

Information campaigns about female earnings alone are unlikely to close the gap
:::
:::
:::

## Conclusions and What It Means

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**What we found:**

1. CI has a [positive, economically meaningful effect]{.hi} on aspirations for higher education (ATT ≈17–20 pp) — suggestive, not conclusive
2. CI's targeting is [efficient]{.hi}: the most vulnerable children benefit the most — no equity-efficiency trade-off
3. [Girls aspire more than boys]{.hi} despite expecting much lower earnings — the gap is not explained by beliefs about future income
:::

::: {.column width="47%"}
::: {.smaller}
**What it means:**

::: {.keybox}
Holistic programs that [combine material support with socio-emotional development]{.hi-gold} are well-positioned to shift aspirations — not just economic constraints
:::

- [Informal attendance requirements]{.hi-gold} may be a valuable design feature — more time in school strengthens educational identity
- Intervening [early (ages 9–12)]{.hi-gold} may be more effective than waiting for aspirations to be fully formed
- Gender-specific barriers require [targeted interventions]{.hi-gold} beyond information provision
:::
:::
:::

## References {.smaller}
